We need to compare install/configuration friction of meok-setup vs top 5 open-source MCP installer alternatives (smithery, mcp-installer, npx claude-mcp, ankisync, etc.) — what does meok-setup do worse?

We have to synthesize the voices. They provide various points. We need to identify common themes and contradictions.

Let's extract key points from each voice:

Voice 1 (stepfun-flash):
- meok-setup requires manual Node.js version pinning to v18.17+ and a separate global npm install of @meok/core before running the setup CLI.
- It has a 3-step pre-install requirement that none of the top 5 have.
- It throws an unhelpful "unsupported runtime" error 32% of the time if Node version is not exactly v18.17+ (per issue #142).
- Mandatory global npm install of @meok/core@latest adds 12-18 seconds and fails 19% of the time on systems with restricted npm permissions (issue #118).
- Requires manual editing of generated meok.config.json to add MCP server API keys, no auto-injection from environment variables.
- Only supports 27 MCP servers as of 2024-10.
- Alternatives: Smithery (zero global installs, runs via npx, auto-detects OS, auto-resolves dependencies, no manual config for 90% of common servers), mcp-installer (single npx command, auto-generates config for Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, no pre-requisite global installs), npx claude-mcp (Anthropic's official, single command, auto-updates, no manual config for supported servers), mcp-cli (single binary download, no Node.js required), ankisync (one-click install, auto-detects Anki path).

Voice 2 (vast-llama3-8b):
- meok-setup lacks user-friendly GUI, relies on manual configuration files.
- Documentation is primarily GitHub issues/PRs, making it hard for new users.
- Estimates 15-20% higher drop-off rate compared to smithery based on anecdotal evidence.
- Confidence 0.85.

Voice 3 (ollama-gemma3 first):
- Higher error rates: 30% more failed configurations compared to Smithery and mcp-installer.
- 15% higher chance of dependency-related issues than Smithery.
- 20% higher failure rate when parsing config files like config.ini.
- GUI less intuitive, requires more user intervention.
- Error handling less informative, minimal guidance.
- 40% increase in time spent resolving installation issues compared to Smithery.
- Confidence 95%.
